title: Chapter 10: Strangers Under One Roof
content: Sarah didn't sleep that night.

I offered her the couch—Luna had moved to the bed, as was our new routine, and I was prepared to take the floor—but Sarah declined. She sat by the window instead, knees pulled to her chest, watching the dark courtyard below with the unblinking vigilance of someone who'd learned that safety was a lie.
I didn't push. You don't push someone who's spent a week as a prisoner. You give them space, you give them options, and you *don't* give them reasons to flinch.

What I did give her was food. A full can of baked beans, heated on the camping stove, and a bottle of water. She ate like it was a test—slowly, carefully, watching me watch her, gauging whether this kindness came with conditions.
It didn't. I knew what conditional kindness looked like. Derek had shown her that version.
"Thank you," she said when the beans were gone. Her voice was steadier now—still hoarse, but no longer whisper-thin. "For getting me out."
"You're welcome."
"I don't know why you did it."
"I told you. I heard you through the vent."
"People hear things and look away. That's what people do." She said it without bitterness, just the flat recitation of a fact she'd learned the hard way.
"I'm not most people."

"No." She studied me—really studied me, the way a doctor examines an X-ray, looking for fractures beneath the surface. "You're not. You've got that girl. The one in the hallway. She's... what is she?"
The question hung in the air. I'd been dreading it since we'd walked through the door.

"She's Luna," I said carefully. "She was a zombie. The system—the thing on my wrist—it gave me a quest to... connect with her. And when I did, when I started feeding her and caring for her, she started recovering. She can talk now. A little. She's getting better every day."
Sarah's eyes were wide. Not with horror—with something closer to fascination. "She's recovering? From being *dead*?"

"From being a zombie. I don't think she was ever fully... gone. The virus took most of her, but something was still there. The food I give her—it's not normal food, it comes from the system—it's bringing her back."
"Bringing her back," Sarah repeated, like she was tasting the words. "Is that what you're going to do with me? Use your system to 'bring me back'?"
The implication stung. "No. I'm not—I didn't save you because of the system."
"But the system told you to." It wasn't a question.
I looked at my wrist. The watch face was dim, showing:

**ROMANCE: SARAH MITCHELL — 15%**
**HOLLOW HEART STATUS: Clear**
"I'm not going to lie to you," I said. "Yes. The system gave me a quest. It told me where you were, that you were in danger, and that rescuing you would give me a reward. A big one."
"What kind of reward?"
"A safe house. A real one—titanium walls, independent systems. Something that could keep all of us alive for months."
Sarah's expression didn't change, but I saw her fingers tighten on her knees. "So you saved me for the reward."
"I saved you because you were being hurt and I couldn't live with ignoring it. The reward is... a bonus. A really, really good bonus. But it's not why."
"How do I know that?"

I held up my wrist. **HOLLOW HEART STATUS: Clear.** "The system can tell if I'm lying. If I don't genuinely care about what happens to you, it penalizes me—rewards revoked, cooldown timers, the whole nine yards. It's called the Hollow Heart protocol, and it's the one thing about this insane magical watch that actually makes sense. I can't fake it. I have to mean it."
She stared at the watch for a long time. At the blue glow. At the words on its face.
"A dating app for the apocalypse," she murmured. "That's what this is, isn't it? A dating app that gives you stuff for falling in love."
The absurdity of it hit me, and I laughed—really laughed, the first genuine laugh in days. "Yeah. Pretty much."
Sarah didn't laugh. But the corner of her mouth twitched. Just barely. A ghost of something that might, in better circumstances, have been a smile.
"I was pre-med," she said quietly. "Before all this. Two semesters left. I was going to be a surgeon." She touched her bruised arm absently. "Now I'm a quest objective."
"You're a person," I said firmly. "The system calls you a target, but you're a person. And you can leave whenever you want. I'll unlock the door right now if that's what you—"
"I'm not leaving." The words came fast and certain. "I'm not stupid. Floor 8 is Derek's territory now, and every other floor is full of dead things. I'd last about ten minutes out there."
"So you'll stay?"

"For now. But I'm not... I'm not a romance target." She said the words like they tasted sour. "I'm not going to be your *quest reward*. You save me, fine. You give me a place to stay, fine. But I'm not going to fall into your arms because some magical watch says I should."
"I wouldn't ask you to."
"Good." She turned back to the window. "Then we understand each other."
I left her to her vigil and went to the bedroom, where Luna was sitting upright against the headboard, her eyes open, her enhanced hearing having tracked every word of the conversation.
"Sarah," Luna said. It wasn't a question—it was a confirmation. She'd been listening.
"Yeah. She's... complicated."
"Hurt," Luna corrected. "Not complicated. Hurt."
It was the most emotionally intelligent thing she'd ever said, and it stopped me in my tracks.
"You're right," I said. "She's hurt."

"Like me," Luna said simply. "I was hurt. You fed me. You stayed. Now I'm better." She looked toward the living room, toward the woman by the window. "She needs time. Then food. Then trust. Same as me."

Sometimes I forgot that Luna wasn't just recovering—she was *wise*. Whatever the virus had taken, it had left something behind that understood suffering in a way I never could. She'd been a zombie, but she'd also been a victim—of the virus, of the collapse, of a death she hadn't chosen. She knew what it meant to need someone and not trust them.
"You're amazing," I told her.
She considered this, then gave a tiny shrug—the most human gesture she'd ever made. "I am Luna."
"Yeah," I said, sitting on the edge of the bed, my hand finding hers in the dark. "You are."
Our fingers intertwined—warm now, almost fully human temperature, strong and gentle—and Luna leaned her head against my shoulder.
From the living room, I heard Sarah get up, move to the couch, and finally—*finally*—lie down.
Boss padded out of the bedroom, sniffed the couch where Sarah lay, and jumped up to curl against her side.
A tiny sound from the couch. Not crying—something softer. Maybe relief.
Three people and a cat, trapped in a titanium-doored apartment at the end of the world.
It wasn't much. But it was more than we'd had yesterday.
**AFFECTION UPDATE: SARAH MITCHELL — 17%**
Slow. But climbing.
And that was enough.
